GlobalPolicy.module-level-focus: Fast deterministic checks for config parsing, auth, TLS material loading, SOCKS5 parsing, exact reply mapping, transport selection, state transitions, effect dispatch, and adapter cleanup.
GlobalPolicy.wave-level-focus: Checks only merged surfaces touched by the current wave, especially sequential fallback, bounded queue behavior, adapter contract enforcement, sliding-window burst detection, and proxy-path integration.
GlobalPolicy.phase-level-focus: Broader transport, reconnect, shutdown, backpressure, burst observability, and regression confidence.
GlobalPolicy.live-wave-focus: For the first two-host live wave, prove remote server readiness, remote client readiness, one deterministic SOCKS5 smoke request, one deterministic auth rejection, and clean shutdown with host-local evidence captured on both machines.
GlobalPolicy.managed-wave-focus: For Phase-9 and later managed-service waves, prove deploy layout alignment, service readiness, restart recovery, repeated smoke behavior, bounded log retention, and redacted secret handling under the same governed runtime anchors.
GlobalPolicy.redaction: Never log secrets, raw tokens, proxy credentials, TLS private keys, or proxied traffic payloads.
GlobalPolicy.remote-operator-rule: Credentials, passwords, and raw host access details stay outside repository artifacts; verification commands must consume them via operator-provided environment variables or an out-of-band secure channel.
GlobalPolicy.manual-docs-driven-rule: When a governed module is documentation-only, workflow-only, or evidence-only and has no repo-local test file, its test-files block points at docs/verification-plan.xml as the canonical review anchor and the real checks live in module-checks plus scenarios.
GlobalPolicy.substantive-test-governance-rule: When a repo-local test file carries multiple scenarios, custom fixtures, or cross-module verification logic, it should include MODULE_CONTRACT, MODULE_MAP, and CHANGE_SUMMARY markup so future agents can navigate and trust the evidence surface quickly.
GlobalPolicy.systemd-evidence-rule: Managed-service verification must capture both service-manager evidence such as systemctl or journalctl state and the underlying runtime anchors emitted by n0wss itself.
GlobalPolicy.evidence-packet-separation-rule: Success-path, restart-recovery, and bad-auth-path artifacts must remain separable so later agents can identify the first divergent block without replaying the entire wave.
GlobalPolicy.doc-script-alignment-rule: When deploy scripts, unit files, or operator docs change, the verification surface must assert that names, paths, and operator commands still match across all three artifacts.
GlobalPolicy.release-tag-alignment-rule: When a release-prep phase is active, the prepared changelog version, the remote tag baseline, and the intended next tag must agree before publication.
GlobalPolicy.release-clean-tree-rule: The release gate must distinguish between allowed controller-owned release-prep edits that are still in progress and the final clean-tree condition required before tag creation.
GlobalPolicy.patch-release-scope-rule: When the next release is a patch over an already published baseline, the changelog and release wording must stay bounded to the post-baseline correction scope and must not silently imply a new runtime feature wave.
GlobalPolicy.release-baseline-truth-rule: A baseline release that exists mainly to publish local GRACE progress must distinguish proven Telegram Desktop TCP or SOCKS5 behavior from still-open Telegram calls or UDP capability gaps and must not publish wishful future scope as current support.
GlobalPolicy.telegram-compat-boundary-rule: Telegram verification may prove only standard SOCKS5 client compatibility over the governed n0wss tunnel and must not silently escalate into claims of universal unblock capability or Telegram-specific protocol support.
GlobalPolicy.manual-client-evidence-rule: When a live wave depends on a third-party interactive client, the verification packet must capture app-side action, n0wss-side anchored logs, socket state, and a first-divergent-block summary so another agent can debug without repeating blind manual steps.
GlobalPolicy.local-forward-proof-rule: When a verified local-workstation scenario reaches a remote localhost listener through SSH forwarding, the verification contract must require explicit evidence that the forward command, forwarded bind, and downstream managed listener were all present during the app-side attempt.
GlobalPolicy.fresh-host-baseline-rule: When a live acceptance wave starts on freshly rebuilt hosts, verification must capture OS identity, SSH reachability, and baseline tool presence before any deploy or app-side interpretation occurs.
GlobalPolicy.operator-handoff-readiness-rule: A manual operator handoff may begin only after governed readiness evidence proves the remote server service, remote client service, remote SOCKS5 listener, and local SSH-forward prerequisites are all green.
GlobalPolicy.media-scenario-separation-rule: Photo transfer, large-file transfer, voice call, video call, reconnect, and idle-resume observations must remain separable in the acceptance packet so a later agent can isolate the first divergent media path.
GlobalPolicy.final-go-no-go-boundary-rule: The final go or no-go decision must stay bounded to the exact tested Telegram Desktop setup, host pair, and operator workflow, and must not silently generalize to mobile clients or universal censorship-resilience claims.
GlobalPolicy.live-required-fields: hostRole, hostIp, processId, logPath, socketAddress, correlationId
GlobalPolicy.foundation-rule: Before Phase-2 execution, every Phase-1 module must have one deterministic success check, one deterministic failure check, and at least one stable log marker anchored to a semantic block.

VF-001.NAME: ClientBootstrap
VF-001.scenario: The client starts successfully, exposes a local SOCKS5 endpoint, and initializes lifecycle plus observability control.
VF-001.expected-outcome: The process starts in client mode with validated config, initialized tracing, active limits, active metrics, active burst detection, and a ready local ingress surface.

VF-002.NAME: SuccessfulProxyRequest
VF-002.scenario: A local SOCKS5 client sends a CONNECT request and receives a valid response through a resolved transport stream.
VF-002.expected-outcome: The request reaches the remote target and returns through the tunnel without deadlocking or leaking session state.

VF-003.NAME: TransportFallback
VF-003.scenario: Iroh stream creation fails or times out and TransportSelector falls back to WSS sequentially.
VF-003.expected-outcome: The fallback succeeds cleanly, only one active transport owner remains, and ProxyBridge still receives a generic ResolvedStream.

VF-004.NAME: RejectedHandshake
VF-004.scenario: A client presents invalid credentials or an unauthorized handshake.
VF-004.expected-outcome: The request is rejected, no active proxy session exists, and logs contain redacted evidence only.

VF-005.NAME: FastQueueFailure
VF-005.scenario: Incoming proxy requests exceed bounded queue capacity.
VF-005.expected-outcome: The request receives a client-visible SOCKS5 failure reply quickly, metrics increment, and sliding-window burst metrics capture the spike.

VF-006.NAME: ExactPolicyAndTargetReplies
VF-006.scenario: Policy-denied and target-unreachable failures are mapped to distinct SOCKS5 reply codes.
VF-006.expected-outcome: Policy failures map to 0x02, target failures map to 0x04 or 0x05 when appropriate, and infrastructure failures remain 0x01.

VF-007.NAME: PostPumpFailureNoReply
VF-007.scenario: A stream pump fails after a successful SOCKS5 success reply has already been sent.
VF-007.expected-outcome: No second reply is attempted; the transport path closes cleanly and the failure remains observable only in logs and metrics.

VF-008.NAME: AdapterCleanupContract
VF-008.scenario: A transport adapter is cancelled, dropped, or forced through an error path while opening a stream.
VF-008.expected-outcome: The adapter returns Err after cancel, leaks no tracked tasks or streams, and violations are surfaced as contract failures.

VF-009.NAME: SlidingWindowBurstDetection
VF-009.scenario: A short rejection spike occurs within a sub-second interval.
VF-009.expected-outcome: Peak rejection rate is captured for the configured alert window, logs stay rate-limited, and the spike is distinguishable from slower rejection patterns.

VF-010.NAME: ControlledShutdown
VF-010.scenario: The process shuts down while active sessions and bridge workers exist.
VF-010.expected-outcome: Listeners stop, sessions drain cleanly, transports close, adapter trackers empty out, and no resources remain leaked.

VF-LIVE-001.NAME: LiveServerReady
VF-LIVE-001.scenario: The remote server host starts n0wss in server mode with real TLS material and a reachable WSS listener.
VF-LIVE-001.expected-outcome: The server validates config, loads TLS, binds the listener, and leaves stable startup evidence without exposing secrets.

VF-LIVE-002.NAME: LiveClientReady
VF-LIVE-002.scenario: The remote client host starts n0wss in client mode against the real remote WSS endpoint and exposes a local SOCKS5 listener.
VF-LIVE-002.expected-outcome: The client validates config, initializes observability, and exposes the local SOCKS5 ingress surface with traceable startup evidence.

VF-LIVE-003.NAME: LiveFirstProxyRequest
VF-LIVE-003.scenario: A SOCKS5-aware client on the remote client host sends one deterministic outbound request through the live tunnel.
VF-LIVE-003.expected-outcome: The request traverses client SOCKS5 ingress, transport selection, server-side auth and WSS handling, and returns a successful response with stable host-local evidence on both machines.

VF-LIVE-004.NAME: LiveAuthReject
VF-LIVE-004.scenario: The remote client host retries the live connection with intentionally invalid auth material.
VF-LIVE-004.expected-outcome: The server rejects the handshake, creates no active session, emits only redacted evidence, and the client observes a deterministic failure.

VF-LIVE-005.NAME: LiveControlledShutdown
VF-LIVE-005.scenario: The live client and live server processes are shut down after the smoke scenarios complete.
VF-LIVE-005.expected-outcome: Listeners stop, live sessions drain, adapter trackers empty, and both hosts retain enough evidence to diagnose a leak or hung shutdown.

VF-DEPLOY-001.NAME: ManagedDeployLayout
VF-DEPLOY-001.scenario: A managed deployment or update stages the governed artifact into the canonical on-host layout.
VF-DEPLOY-001.expected-outcome: The target hosts receive one approved artifact identity, canonical paths, and bounded evidence without raw secret leakage.

VF-DEPLOY-002.NAME: ManagedServerServiceReady
VF-DEPLOY-002.scenario: The managed server systemd service starts with the governed runtime shape and reaches readiness.
VF-DEPLOY-002.expected-outcome: The service becomes active, emits startup anchors, and binds the expected WSS listener.

VF-DEPLOY-003.NAME: ManagedClientServiceReady
VF-DEPLOY-003.scenario: The managed client systemd service starts and exposes the governed SOCKS5 endpoint.
VF-DEPLOY-003.expected-outcome: The service becomes active, emits startup anchors, and binds the expected local SOCKS5 listener.

VF-DEPLOY-004.NAME: ManagedRestartRecovery
VF-DEPLOY-004.scenario: The operator restarts or updates a managed service and proves recovery without stale listeners.
VF-DEPLOY-004.expected-outcome: A new service process reaches readiness, stale listeners disappear, and reconnect-capable flows still work.

VF-DEPLOY-005.NAME: ManagedRepeatedSmoke
VF-DEPLOY-005.scenario: Repeated deterministic SOCKS5 smoke requests are run after managed startup.
VF-DEPLOY-005.expected-outcome: Each request succeeds with stable output metadata and the trajectory remains anchored through transport selection and pump completion.

VF-DEPLOY-006.NAME: ManagedBadAuthReject
VF-DEPLOY-006.scenario: A managed bad-auth attempt is executed after the managed success path.
VF-DEPLOY-006.expected-outcome: The server rejects the handshake with redacted evidence, the client sees deterministic failure, and no accepted session survives.

VF-DEPLOY-007.NAME: ManagedLogRotation
VF-DEPLOY-007.scenario: Log rotation executes under the managed deployment shape.
VF-DEPLOY-007.expected-outcome: Old logs are bounded, current logs remain writable, and the active evidence packet stays available for triage.

VF-DEPLOY-008.NAME: SecretHandlingDiscipline
VF-DEPLOY-008.scenario: The managed deployment consumes secret material from approved sources only.
VF-DEPLOY-008.expected-outcome: No repository artifact, reusable transcript, or bounded evidence packet stores raw secrets, while startup still succeeds.

scenarios.scenario-1: Valid startup arguments route execution into the expected runtime mode, build optional TLS bootstrap surfaces, and bind the selected runtime listener until cancellation.
scenarios.scenario-2: Shutdown sequencing stops accepts before draining transports and runtime loops do not exit cleanly before cancellation.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Once shutdown begins, no new accepts or registrations are allowed.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: Client and server runtime launch paths must stay alive until an explicit cancellation boundary is triggered.

V-M-CLI.wave-follow-up: Use CLI orchestration in client and server integration tests.
V-M-CLI.phase-follow-up: Run end-to-end startup and shutdown checks with representative configs.

scenarios.scenario-1: Release build produces a deployable n0wss executable that forwards process arguments into the governed CLI runtime surface.
scenarios.scenario-2: Bootstrap or runtime errors surface as a non-zero process exit without duplicating startup logic outside M-CLI.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The release artifact includes a shell-executable binary at target/release/n0wss.

V-M-CLI-BIN.wave-follow-up: Use the built executable for local release smoke and live host staging.
V-M-CLI-BIN.phase-follow-up: Keep packaging verification in sync with any future CLI entrypoint refactors.

scenarios.scenario-1: Valid client and server configuration parses into a stable typed config including limits, timeouts, optional client TLS trust overrides, reply policy, retry settings, and burst thresholds.
scenarios.scenario-2: Invalid bind addresses, TLS paths, or limit values fail with deterministic errors.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Invalid configuration must not progress into gateway, session, or listener startup.

V-M-CONFIG.wave-follow-up: Use validated config in fallback, bounded queue, timeout, retry, and burst-window tests.
V-M-CONFIG.phase-follow-up: Run the full test suite with representative client and server configs.

scenarios.scenario-1: Tracing, counters, gauges, histograms, and sliding-window burst metrics initialize with stable field propagation and redaction rules.
scenarios.scenario-2: Burst spikes update peak-rate gauges correctly while logs remain rate-limited and non-bursty traffic does not trigger alerts.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Metrics reflect queue-full, session-limit, and reply-code outcomes accurately.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: Sliding-window burst detection distinguishes short spikes from slow rejection patterns.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-3: Peak-rate gauges reset on the configured maintenance interval.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-4: Burst thresholds remain documented as deployment-time tuning values rather than architectural constants.

V-M-OBS.wave-follow-up: Confirm metrics and aggregation appear in integration path failures.
V-M-OBS.phase-follow-up: Review alert-friendly observability under burst load and tune thresholds with docs/OPERATORS.md.

scenarios.scenario-1: Valid credentials or ticket metadata are accepted.
scenarios.scenario-2: Invalid credentials are rejected and sensitive values are redacted.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Rejected handshakes must not create a session handle.

V-M-AUTH.wave-follow-up: Exercise the auth boundary through the WSS gateway.
V-M-AUTH.phase-follow-up: Review logs to verify no secrets are emitted on failure paths.

scenarios.scenario-1: Valid TLS material loads into client and server contexts.
scenarios.scenario-2: Missing, malformed, expired, or untrusted material fails deterministically.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: TLS load failure must prevent websocket startup.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: Client-mode trust-anchor loading must not require server certificate key material.

V-M-TLS.wave-follow-up: Use TLS contexts inside handshake integration tests.
V-M-TLS.phase-follow-up: Exercise certificate rotation or reload scenarios if added later.

scenarios.scenario-1: A valid TLS plus WSS handshake returns a WSS-backed ResolvedStream.
scenarios.scenario-2: Cancel, drop, and timeout paths satisfy the adapter cleanup contract without leaked tracked tasks.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Gateway must not embed transport-selection logic.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: Contract tests must cover cancel-before-open, cancel-during-open, no-leaked-tasks, and drop-cleanup behavior through the adapter task tracker.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-3: Generic contract tests must include socket-level cleanup checks for partially opened connections.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-4: Governed helper files under src/transport/* that support WSS remain covered even when they are tracked as first-class graph nodes.

V-M-WSS-GATEWAY.wave-follow-up: Use WSS stream creation in fallback and proxy integration tests.
V-M-WSS-GATEWAY.phase-follow-up: Run shutdown and reconnect tests against both client and server modes.

scenarios.scenario-1: The adapter opens a direct or relay-backed stream with stable diagnostics.
scenarios.scenario-2: Cancel, drop, timeout, or transport failure surfaces as a deterministic error and leaks no tracked tasks or partial state.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Transport failure must not leave leaked partial state after cancel or timeout.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: Contract tests must cover cancel-before-open, cancel-during-open, no-leaked-tasks, and drop-cleanup behavior through the adapter task tracker.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-3: Generic contract tests must include socket-level cleanup checks, and adapter-specific tests must cover adapter-owned resource release.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-4: Governed helper files under src/transport/* that support iroh remain covered even when they are tracked as first-class graph nodes.

V-M-IROH-ADAPTER.wave-follow-up: Pair transport setup with selector fallback integration tests.
V-M-IROH-ADAPTER.phase-follow-up: Validate relay fallback behavior in a constrained network environment.

scenarios.scenario-1: TransportSelector chooses iroh first and falls back to WSS sequentially.
scenarios.scenario-2: State transitions, effect dispatch, max-session limits, draining-session rejection, and contract violations remain deterministic.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: State transitions are pure and Closing is one-way toward Closed.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: SessionEffect top-level variant count remains bounded and maps only to target components.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-3: EffectHandler branch count remains stable and new subcommands do not change its top-level routing structure.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-4: SessionRegistry reserve and remove semantics free capacity deterministically.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-5: RegistryCommand and TimerCommand variant counts remain within locally defined hygiene limits.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-6: SessionManager rejects new stream resolution once a session stops accepting new streams, without invoking transport adapters.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-7: Governed helper files under src/session/* remain covered by this session verification surface even when they are tracked as first-class graph nodes.

V-M-SESSION.wave-follow-up: Connect session lifecycle to proxy, fallback, and shutdown tests.
V-M-SESSION.phase-follow-up: Verify clean shutdown under repeated reconnect attempts.

scenarios.scenario-1: A valid SOCKS5 CONNECT request is parsed into a normalized ProxyIntent.
scenarios.scenario-2: Malformed requests, saturated intent queue, policy denies, and target failures map to deterministic client-visible replies.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Queue saturation must produce a SOCKS5 failure reply in less than 50ms in local tests.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: Policy failures map to 0x02 and target failures map to exact target-oriented reply codes when possible.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-3: PumpFailed returns no SOCKS5 reply because success was already emitted.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-4: ProxyError and TransportError matches remain exhaustive without wildcard arms.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-5: CI blocks any allow-list override that re-enables wildcard enum match arms for reply mapping.

V-M-SOCKS5.wave-follow-up: Feed normalized ProxyIntent items into bridge integration tests.
V-M-SOCKS5.phase-follow-up: Exercise concurrent local clients under listener saturation.

scenarios.scenario-1: A valid ProxyIntent is tunneled successfully over a resolved generic stream.
scenarios.scenario-2: Remote disconnect, total timeout, or drain shutdown does not deadlock the client side.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: ProxyBridge must not import WssGateway or IrohAdapter directly.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: Failure paths must close both stream directions and notify SessionManager.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-3: Total request timeout must bound client-visible failure latency.

V-M-PROXY-BRIDGE.wave-follow-up: Exercise SOCKS5 traffic over the full tunnel path.
V-M-PROXY-BRIDGE.phase-follow-up: Run prolonged transfer, backpressure, and reconnect checks.

scenarios.scenario-1: The governed crate root exposes the implemented module surface without stale exports or placeholder metadata.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The crate root contract, module map, and re-export surface stay synchronized with the implemented modules.

V-M-CRATE-ROOT.wave-follow-up: Keep the crate root export list aligned with future module additions and removals.
V-M-CRATE-ROOT.phase-follow-up: Review crate-root metadata whenever the governed module surface changes.

scenarios.scenario-1: The governed transport module re-exports the adapter contract, stream contract, and task tracker helpers without introducing transport logic.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The transport module stays a logic-free aggregator while its exported helpers remain covered by transport-boundary verification.

V-M-TRANSPORT-MOD.wave-follow-up: Keep this wrapper synchronized whenever transport helper modules are added, removed, or renamed.
V-M-TRANSPORT-MOD.phase-follow-up: Review the transport module export surface whenever transport-boundary tests or helper contracts change.

scenarios.scenario-1: README quickstart and changelog align with the implemented CLI and test surfaces.
scenarios.scenario-2: Stale commands, stale file paths, or stale configuration flags are rejected during docs review.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Release docs reference only paths and commands that exist in the repository.

V-M-RELEASE-DOCS.assertion-note: Manual/docs-driven verification uses this verification entry as the canonical review anchor because no repo-local test file exists for the docs surface.
V-M-RELEASE-DOCS.wave-follow-up: Use release docs in manual smoke testing.
V-M-RELEASE-DOCS.phase-follow-up: Update changelog and quickstart at each release cut.

scenarios.scenario-1: GitHub CI mirrors the required local checks for release readiness.
scenarios.scenario-2: Workflow drift, stale commands, or missing release metadata are caught before publication.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Workflow commands must match the repository's current release gates.

V-M-GITHUB-RELEASE.assertion-note: Manual/docs-driven verification uses this verification entry as the canonical review anchor because workflow validation is command- and artifact-driven rather than test-file-driven.
V-M-GITHUB-RELEASE.wave-follow-up: Run the workflow on a branch before cutting a release tag.
V-M-GITHUB-RELEASE.phase-follow-up: Keep release automation source-first until binary packaging is stabilized.

scenarios.scenario-1: A narrow smoke run proves startup, one proxied request, and graceful shutdown.
scenarios.scenario-2: Release-candidate regressions fail before external/manual testing begins.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The smoke gate must remain narrow and deterministic enough for release use.

V-M-SMOKE-E2E.wave-follow-up: Extend smoke coverage only after the first external test wave stabilizes.
V-M-SMOKE-E2E.phase-follow-up: Use smoke evidence before GitHub release publication.

scenarios.scenario-1: Exactly one host is fixed as the live server and one host is fixed as the live client with stable ports and file paths.
scenarios.scenario-2: Missing or ambiguous host-role information blocks the live wave before deployment starts.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Repository artifacts must never contain raw SSH passwords or unredacted auth tokens.

V-M-LIVE-HOST-INVENTORY.wave-follow-up: Carry the same frozen inventory through all live-wave commands and evidence packets.
V-M-LIVE-HOST-INVENTORY.phase-follow-up: Move host inventory to more durable operator automation if live waves become routine.

scenarios.scenario-1: The same release artifact and runtime layout are staged on both hosts.
scenarios.scenario-2: Missing binary, wrong path, or inconsistent staging shape blocks the live wave before startup.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The binary identity or checksum must match across both hosts for one live wave.

V-M-LIVE-BUILD-PACK.wave-follow-up: Keep the staging layout stable until the first live gate is closed.
V-M-LIVE-BUILD-PACK.phase-follow-up: Replace ad hoc staging with repeatable packaging once the live wave is proven.

scenarios.scenario-1: The live server has usable TLS material and the client targets the matching endpoint identity.
scenarios.scenario-2: Missing files, trust mismatch, or endpoint identity mismatch blocks live startup before smoke execution.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The first live wave terminates TLS directly in n0wss and must not silently depend on an undocumented reverse proxy.

V-M-LIVE-TLS-BOOTSTRAP.wave-follow-up: Confirm the client trust shape before the first live success-path run.
V-M-LIVE-TLS-BOOTSTRAP.phase-follow-up: Promote the TLS bootstrap into reusable operator documentation after the first live phase passes.

scenarios.scenario-1: The remote server host emits config, TLS, and startup anchors and binds the live WSS listener.
scenarios.scenario-2: The remote server host fails before readiness and provides enough evidence to isolate the first divergent block.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Readiness requires both startup anchors and bound socket proof from the live host.

V-M-LIVE-SERVER-LAUNCH.wave-follow-up: Do not start the live smoke path until the server readiness packet is complete.
V-M-LIVE-SERVER-LAUNCH.phase-follow-up: Turn the launch shape into a service unit only after the first live wave is stable.

scenarios.scenario-1: The remote client host emits config and startup anchors and binds the local SOCKS5 ingress port.
scenarios.scenario-2: The remote client host fails before readiness and leaves enough evidence to isolate the first divergent block.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Client readiness requires both startup anchors and bound SOCKS5 listener proof from the live host.

V-M-LIVE-CLIENT-LAUNCH.wave-follow-up: Do not run the live proxy request until the client readiness packet is complete.
V-M-LIVE-CLIENT-LAUNCH.phase-follow-up: Promote the launch procedure into reusable operator docs only after the first live wave passes.

scenarios.scenario-1: One deterministic SOCKS5-aware request crosses the live tunnel and returns a successful response.
scenarios.scenario-2: Transport, auth, routing, or target failure stops the smoke path with enough evidence to identify the first divergent block.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The first live smoke must stay narrow and deterministic enough to separate tunnel defects from broad load or concurrency issues.

V-M-LIVE-PROXY-SMOKE.wave-follow-up: Run the bad-auth scenario only after a clean success-path evidence packet exists.
V-M-LIVE-PROXY-SMOKE.phase-follow-up: Expand beyond one smoke request only after the first live gate is green.

scenarios.scenario-1: The live wave captures bounded high-signal logs, socket state, and smoke output from both hosts.
scenarios.scenario-2: Evidence capture is missing, too noisy, or too secret-heavy to support a follow-up fix.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Evidence packets must stay small enough for follow-up analysis and must redact secrets.

V-M-LIVE-OBS-EVIDENCE.assertion-note: Manual/docs-driven verification uses this verification entry as the canonical review anchor because the evidence surface is produced on remote hosts rather than by a repo-local test file.
V-M-LIVE-OBS-EVIDENCE.wave-follow-up: Reuse the same evidence layout for both success and failure packets in the first live phase.
V-M-LIVE-OBS-EVIDENCE.phase-follow-up: Move the evidence procedure into stable operator runbooks after the first live phase passes.

scenarios.scenario-1: Every failed live attempt is condensed into a GRACE failure packet before rerun approval.
scenarios.scenario-2: A live rerun is attempted without identifying the first divergent block and next action.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: No repeat live attempt is approved until the first divergent block is identified from bounded evidence.

V-M-LIVE-FAILURE-TRIAGE.wave-follow-up: Route live failures into grace-fix or a human repair plan rather than blind retries.
V-M-LIVE-FAILURE-TRIAGE.phase-follow-up: Keep the failure packet template stable across later live phases.

scenarios.scenario-1: The deployment layout is explicit, role-aware, and consistent across scripts, services, and operator documentation.
scenarios.scenario-2: Conflicting paths or ownership assumptions make deploy or recovery behavior ambiguous.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Every managed host role must resolve to one canonical binary path, one canonical secret path policy, and one canonical log path policy.

V-M-DEPLOY-LAYOUT.wave-follow-up: Reuse the same layout during deploy scripting and systemd verification.
V-M-DEPLOY-LAYOUT.phase-follow-up: Do not introduce alternate ad hoc layouts after Phase-9 is approved.

scenarios.scenario-1: The operator workflow defines approved secret sources and redaction discipline without embedding raw secret values.
scenarios.scenario-2: Plaintext secrets remain part of the standard deployment or evidence workflow.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Verification evidence for Phase-9 must describe secret locations or source types without reproducing raw secret contents.

V-M-SECRET-HYGIENE.wave-follow-up: Apply the same secret-discipline rules to Live Wave 2 and later production-like runs.
V-M-SECRET-HYGIENE.phase-follow-up: Document token rotation and secret replacement as a required operator action.

scenarios.scenario-1: The deploy script stages the governed artifact and switches over to the canonical layout repeatably.
scenarios.scenario-2: Partial copy, unsafe overwrite, or inconsistent target paths leave the host in an ambiguous state.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Deploy evidence must show which artifact identity was staged and whether switchover completed.

V-M-DEPLOY-SCRIPTING.wave-follow-up: Use the script as the only approved path for Live Wave 2 staging.
V-M-DEPLOY-SCRIPTING.phase-follow-up: Add rollback-aware deploy checks before broader operator handoff.

scenarios.scenario-1: Managed services start, stop, and restart the correct runtime surface with canonical paths.
scenarios.scenario-2: Broken ExecStart, missing env files, or restart loops block managed launch readiness.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Service-managed runtime must remain observable through the same core startup and shutdown anchors as manual launches.

V-M-SYSTEMD-UNITS.wave-follow-up: Validate service readiness on both hosts before repeated managed smoke checks.
V-M-SYSTEMD-UNITS.phase-follow-up: Keep unit naming, env-source names, and log targets stable across updates.

scenarios.scenario-1: Log rotation bounds retained evidence while preserving active log continuity.
scenarios.scenario-2: Rotation truncates the active evidence packet or leaves the service unable to write logs.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Current-wave evidence must remain readable until the operator closes the wave.

V-M-LOG-ROTATION.wave-follow-up: Run one rotation dry run before Live Wave 2 evidence collection.
V-M-LOG-ROTATION.phase-follow-up: Keep rotation sizing aligned with bounded-evidence expectations.

scenarios.scenario-1: The runbook covers install, start, stop, update, restart evidence, rollback, and bounded failure triage.
scenarios.scenario-2: Operator docs drift from the real deploy scripts, services, or evidence commands.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Every operator action used in Live Wave 2 must map to one documented command path and one evidence path.

V-M-OPERATOR-RUNBOOK.wave-follow-up: Use the runbook directly during Live Wave 2 to prove docs and execution stay aligned.
V-M-OPERATOR-RUNBOOK.phase-follow-up: Keep runbook updates in the same change set as deploy or service workflow changes.

scenarios.scenario-1: The managed services pass repeated smoke, restart recovery, and bad-auth verification with bounded evidence.
scenarios.scenario-2: Managed startup, restart, reconnect, or reject-path evidence diverges before the second live gate closes.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Live Wave 2 must separate success-path, restart-recovery, and bad-auth evidence into bounded packets rather than one conflated transcript.

V-M-LIVE-WAVE-2.wave-follow-up: Escalate any managed-service failure into a GRACE failure packet before reruns.
V-M-LIVE-WAVE-2.phase-follow-up: Use this wave as the gate before any broader production-like rollout.

scenarios.scenario-1: All substantive governed test files carry committed GRACE headers and no longer block release tagging through a dirty tree.
scenarios.scenario-2: Prepared test markup remains uncommitted or drifts beyond the approved governance scope.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Release preparation must not proceed while the governed test-file cleanup step still leaves tracked test files modified.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The cleanup commit must be limited to the approved substantive test files and their GRACE headers.

V-M-TEST-GOVERNANCE-SYNC.wave-follow-up: Commit only the governed markup changes; do not mix unrelated runtime edits into this cleanup step.

module-checks.check-1: rg -n 'still being finalized|first public release' README.md docs/OPERATORS.md

scenarios.scenario-1: Release-facing docs reflect the completed managed deployment posture and source-first release shape without stale language.
scenarios.scenario-2: README or operator docs still claim deployment is unfinished after Phase-9 is closed.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: README and operator docs must describe the same current release posture.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: README must no longer claim that public deployment flow is still being finalized once Phase-9 is done.

scenarios.scenario-1: CHANGELOG.md contains a v0.3.0 entry that matches the implemented Phase-9 and managed live-wave scope.
scenarios.scenario-2: The next release entry is missing, mis-numbered, or omits the managed deployment surface.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The changelog version prepared for release must match the next remote tag after v0.2.0.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The v0.3.0 entry must mention the managed deployment surface and the second managed live wave.

scenarios.scenario-1: The repository reaches a clean release-prep state with green release checks and synchronized GRACE artifacts.
scenarios.scenario-2: Any governed tree dirtiness, failing check, or artifact drift blocks the v0.3.0 tag.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Release gate evidence must include clippy, cargo test, review, refresh, and status outputs or their summarized results.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The final tag-ready state requires no tracked dirty files after the release-prep commits land.

V-M-RELEASE-GATE-0-3-0.phase-follow-up: Do not create the v0.3.0 tag until this gate closes green.

scenarios.scenario-1: The existing GitHub release workflow remains aligned with a tag-driven source release for v0.3.0.
scenarios.scenario-2: Workflow behavior or changelog mapping drifts from the prepared release tag path.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The next release tag after the remote v0.2.0 baseline is v0.3.0.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The workflow must still publish CHANGELOG.md as the source-first release body for the prepared tag.

module-checks.check-2: rg -n 'custom protocol|universal unblock|all blocking|guaranteed unblock' README.md docs/OPERATORS.md docs/verification-plan.xml || true

scenarios.scenario-1: Telegram compatibility is documented as a standard SOCKS5 client profile over the existing n0wss tunnel.
scenarios.scenario-2: Documentation implies a new Telegram-specific protocol, overclaims unblock guarantees, or leaves the SOCKS5 profile underspecified.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Telegram support must remain framed as standard SOCKS5 client compatibility, not a custom client protocol.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: Profile documentation must state the bounded claim surface clearly enough that later agents do not confuse Telegram compatibility with VPN-style whole-device tunneling.

scenarios.scenario-1: The runbook defines a bounded operator path for exposing n0wss as a Telegram-facing SOCKS5 proxy.
scenarios.scenario-2: Telegram-facing readiness is left implicit, misses the governed local SOCKS5 bind assumptions, or omits the managed-service preflight checks.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The readiness path must include a local listener proof for the governed SOCKS5 port before any Telegram app-side action is attempted.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The readiness path must be expressible as a bounded operator sequence rather than undocumented interactive intuition.

scenarios.scenario-1: Telegram can connect through the local SOCKS5 endpoint and survives one managed reconnect cycle.
scenarios.scenario-2: Telegram connectivity fails or reconnect diverges despite a green generic SOCKS5 tunnel path.
scenarios.scenario-3: Telegram app-side settings are wrong, and the evidence must stop at readiness or SOCKS5 ingress instead of guessing at tunnel failure.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Telegram-specific evidence must include one initial connect packet and one reconnect packet rather than one conflated log dump.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: When the Telegram app attempt does not produce SOCKS5 ingress markers, the failure packet must remain classified as app-side or readiness-side until the tunnel path is actually exercised.

module-checks.check-1: rg -n 'Telegram|first divergent block|SOCKS5|readiness packet|connect packet|reconnect packet|failure packet' docs/verification-plan.xml docs/OPERATORS.md

scenarios.scenario-1: Telegram-specific evidence packets distinguish app-side misconfiguration from n0wss tunnel failure.
scenarios.scenario-2: Telegram-specific debugging still requires blind reruns because evidence is not separated cleanly.
scenarios.scenario-3: A local Telegram relaunch attempt diverges before stable UI recovery, but the packet still preserves whether the tunnel path itself emitted fresh SOCKS5, transport, and handshake evidence.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Telegram evidence must separate readiness, connect, reconnect, and failure packets.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: Each packet must preserve the minimum cross-host fields needed for handoff: app-side action summary, local listener state, anchored client log tail, anchored server log tail when applicable, and first divergent block.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-3: A failed local Telegram relaunch must be recorded as an app-launch divergence if fresh tunnel markers already prove the reconnect path, rather than being misclassified as transport failure.

module-checks.check-2: rg -n 'all blocking|any blocking|guaranteed|always bypass|universal unblock' README.md docs/OPERATORS.md docs/verification-plan.xml || true

scenarios.scenario-1: Public-facing docs state only the Telegram compatibility that is actually verified.
scenarios.scenario-2: Docs overclaim universal unblock capability beyond the verified SOCKS5 compatibility evidence.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Compatibility language must not silently escalate from proxy compatibility to guaranteed unblock claims.

module-checks.check-1: rg -n 'ssh -N -L 127.0.0.1:1080:127.0.0.1:1080|Telegram Desktop|local workstation|remote client host' docs/OPERATORS.md docs/development-plan.xml

scenarios.scenario-1: The operator runbook explicitly distinguishes the local-workstation Telegram Desktop scenario from Telegram running directly on the remote client host.
scenarios.scenario-2: The runbook still implies that 127.0.0.1:1080 is locally available without an SSH forward in the verified Desktop scenario.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The local-workstation Desktop path must show the exact SSH local-forward shape before Telegram is pointed at 127.0.0.1:1080.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The runbook must also preserve the separate remote-host variant so the SSH-forward step is not falsely universalized.

scenarios.scenario-1: LV-007 and the Telegram evidence packet requirements explicitly encode the local-forward prerequisite for the verified Desktop scenario.
scenarios.scenario-2: Verification commands still omit the forward and therefore do not reproduce the validated Telegram Desktop path.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The Telegram Desktop wave must prove three states for the local-workstation variant: local forwarded bind present, remote managed SOCKS5 listener present, and Telegram pointed at the forwarded local endpoint.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: A later Connecting-only failure must first check forward liveness before classifying a tunnel-side regression.

module-checks.check-1: rg -n 'M-TELEGRAM-FORWARD-|forwarded Desktop|SSH local forward' docs/knowledge-graph.xml docs/development-plan.xml

scenarios.scenario-1: The graph records the clarified Telegram Desktop operator path and its verification dependencies without changing runtime-module meaning.
scenarios.scenario-2: The graph remains silent about the clarified forwarded Desktop path after the plan and verification contracts are updated.

scenarios.scenario-1: The changelog contains a v0.3.1 entry that describes the Telegram forward clarification patch scope accurately.
scenarios.scenario-2: The changelog still tops out at v0.3.0 or describes a larger runtime change than was actually shipped after v0.3.0.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The v0.3.1 entry must explicitly read as a patch release after the already published v0.3.0 baseline.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The notes must stay bounded to the Telegram forward clarification and must not imply a new transport or deployment wave.

scenarios.scenario-1: Release-facing docs remain aligned with the verified Telegram Desktop forwarded path.
scenarios.scenario-2: Release-facing docs contradict the SSH-forward prerequisite or reintroduce stale Telegram operator wording.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Release-facing wording must preserve the SSH-forward prerequisite for the local-workstation Telegram Desktop path.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: Release-facing wording must not imply that v0.3.1 adds a new runtime feature beyond the already verified correction scope.

module-checks.check-5: git rev-parse --verify refs/tags/v0.3.1 >/dev/null 2>&1 && echo local-tag-present || true

scenarios.scenario-1: The release gate confirms a clean tree, synced artifacts, a v0.3.1 changelog entry, and a remote baseline where the next tag is v0.3.1.
scenarios.scenario-2: The tree is dirty, the changelog is stale, or the remote tag baseline no longer justifies v0.3.1.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Before tag creation, the local tree must be clean and patch-integrity checks must pass.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The remote baseline must still show v0.3.0 as the newest published release tag before v0.3.1 is created.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-3: The gate must fail if a stale local v0.3.1 tag already exists before the approved push step.

scenarios.scenario-1: The existing GitHub release workflow remains valid for a pushed v0.3.1 tag.
scenarios.scenario-2: The workflow no longer matches the tag-driven source-release path or the local tag state diverges from the intended push plan.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The push step must target exactly v0.3.1 and rely on the existing tag-driven workflow rather than an ad hoc release path.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The changelog body published by GitHub must come from CHANGELOG.md for the prepared v0.3.1 entry.

scenarios.scenario-1: Both rebuilt Ubuntu hosts are reachable and clean enough to start the final managed deployment wave.
scenarios.scenario-2: One or both hosts are unreachable, not root-accessible, or missing baseline shell tooling needed for the governed deploy path.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The host baseline packet must identify both host roles explicitly before any deploy step begins.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: Fresh-host evidence must include SSH reachability, OS identity, and the presence of baseline shell tooling on both rebuilt machines.

scenarios.scenario-1: The governed managed deployment path is reapplied to both fresh hosts without ad hoc drift.
scenarios.scenario-2: Deployment requires undocumented host mutation or leaves either host without the governed binary and service layout.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The final deploy step must reuse the governed managed deployment path rather than hand-edited host mutations.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The deployed binary, cert layout, and service files must land on both fresh hosts before service readiness can be claimed.

module-checks.check-1: ssh root@$N0WSS_SERVER_HOST 'systemctl is-active n0wss-server && systemctl show n0wss-server -p MainPID --no-pager'
module-checks.check-2: ssh root@$N0WSS_CLIENT_HOST 'systemctl is-active n0wss-client && systemctl show n0wss-client -p MainPID --no-pager'

scenarios.scenario-1: Server and client services are green with bound listeners and startup anchors before the operator app-side test begins.
scenarios.scenario-2: The app-side acceptance test would start before the fresh deployment has proven readiness.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Readiness evidence must include service-manager state and runtime anchors rather than socket state alone.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The operator handoff is forbidden until the remote SOCKS5 listener and WSS service are both proven live.

scenarios.scenario-1: The operator receives one exact and unambiguous handoff path for the final Telegram Desktop test.
scenarios.scenario-2: The final handoff still leaves ambiguity about SSH forwarding, SOCKS5 settings, or when the operator should begin testing.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The handoff must explicitly say when I declare the environment ready and what exact SSH-forward command the operator must run locally.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The handoff must not blur together local-workstation Telegram Desktop and Telegram running directly on the remote client host.

scenarios.scenario-1: The operator performs the final Telegram Desktop acceptance path and bounded evidence proves whether traffic crossed the governed SOCKS5 and tunnel runtime.
scenarios.scenario-2: The operator reports Telegram symptoms but the evidence packet cannot distinguish app-side state, SSH-forward failure, readiness failure, or tunnel/runtime failure.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The acceptance packet must separate basic connect evidence from later media-heavy checks.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: If the operator reports Telegram stuck on Connecting, the first triage step must verify SSH-forward liveness before blaming the tunnel runtime.

scenarios.scenario-1: Photo, large-file, and call-path checks are captured as separable media scenarios rather than being merged into one anecdotal result.
scenarios.scenario-2: A media-heavy Telegram failure leaves no way to tell whether only calls broke, only file upload broke, or the whole tunnel path failed.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: Photo, large-file, voice-call, and video-call observations must remain separately classifiable even if they happen in one manual session.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: Media-heavy failures must record whether the basic connect path remained healthy at the same time.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-3: If calls fail while messages and file transfer remain green, the packet must preserve that divergence as a call-path gap instead of collapsing it into a generic tunnel failure.

module-checks.check-1: rg -n 'first divergent block|readiness packet|acceptance packet|media packet|go-no-go' docs/verification-plan.xml docs/OPERATORS.md

scenarios.scenario-1: The final acceptance packet is bounded and sufficient for handoff without replaying the entire live session.
scenarios.scenario-2: The final live acceptance still depends on prose-only memory or conflated logs instead of bounded evidence packets.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The final packet must contain distinct readiness, basic-acceptance, media, and failure-or-go-no-go sections.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The packet must preserve a first-divergent-block summary for any failed sub-scenario.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-3: The final packet must preserve that March 29 basic and file-transfer paths stayed green while the calls path diverged into a separate failure summary with a later UDP-capable next action.

module-checks.check-1: rg -n 'go/no-go|Telegram Desktop|tested host setup' docs/development-plan.xml README.md docs/verification-plan.xml

scenarios.scenario-1: The final decision is bounded to the tested Telegram Desktop setup on the rebuilt hosts.
scenarios.scenario-2: The final decision overclaims more than the evidence packet can support.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The final decision must state that the evidence covers Telegram Desktop on the rebuilt two-host environment and not a wider client matrix.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The go or no-go outcome must cite readiness, basic-connect, and media-path evidence rather than operator memory alone.

scenarios.scenario-1: The changelog contains a bounded v0.3.2 entry for the current verified local baseline.
scenarios.scenario-2: The changelog omits v0.3.2 or overstates Telegram call support.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The v0.3.2 notes must state that Telegram Desktop text, media, and large-file transfer were verified on the tested setup.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The v0.3.2 notes must state that audio or video calls remain outside the proven envelope and are not released as supported capability.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-3: The changelog wording must read as a baseline publication of current local progress, not as a new UDP-capable feature wave.

scenarios.scenario-1: Release-facing docs reflect the verified Telegram Desktop TCP/SOCKS5 envelope and current call limitation honestly.
scenarios.scenario-2: Docs imply Telegram calls support or omit the fresh-host verified baseline.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: README and operator docs must preserve the verified local-workstation Desktop path through the SSH local forward before Telegram points at 127.0.0.1:1080.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: Release-facing wording must state that calls remain an open gap rather than a flaky supported feature.

scenarios.scenario-1: Shared artifacts remain aligned with the release truth before v0.3.2 tagging.
scenarios.scenario-2: Release truth diverges between plan, verification, graph, and user-facing docs.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The three shared artifacts must all encode Phase-15 and the same set of v0.3.2 release modules before the gate runs.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The synchronized baseline must remain bounded to verified Telegram Desktop TCP or SOCKS5 behavior plus the explicit calls gap.

scenarios.scenario-1: The repository is clean, aligned, and justified for v0.3.2 tagging.
scenarios.scenario-2: Dirty tree, stale notes, or wrong remote baseline blocks v0.3.2.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The release gate must confirm that origin already contains v0.3.1 and that no stale local v0.3.2 tag exists before the publish step.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The release gate must confirm that the tree is clean at tag time, not just during earlier controller-owned release preparation.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-3: The release gate must explicitly reject any release notes or docs that claim Telegram calls support.

scenarios.scenario-1: The existing GitHub workflow is still valid for pushing v0.3.2.
scenarios.scenario-2: Workflow drift would prevent source-release publication for v0.3.2.

required-trace-assertions.assertion-1: The release path must still derive release body content from CHANGELOG.md rather than an unstated ad hoc draft.
required-trace-assertions.assertion-2: The push step must target v0.3.2 exactly and remain compatible with the existing tag-driven workflow.

LV-001.NAME: RemoteAccessPreparation
LV-001.goal: Prepare both clean hosts without storing credentials in repository artifacts.

required-evidence.evidence-1: The same release binary or checksum is staged for both hosts.
required-evidence.evidence-2: Host roles are fixed: one machine serves WSS, the other serves client SOCKS5 ingress.
required-evidence.evidence-3: No command transcript or log artifact stores the SSH password or raw auth token.
required-evidence.evidence-4: The server host exposes bash, ss, and openssl, and the client host exposes bash, ss, and curl before live staging begins.

LV-001.failure-packet-template: scenario=RemoteAccessPreparation; expected-evidence=both hosts reachable and identified; observed-evidence=ssh or staging failure output; first-divergent-block=HOST_CONNECTIVITY; next-action=repair ssh access or package staging before live launch

LV-002.NAME: TwoHostSmokeExecution
LV-002.goal: Run the first deterministic two-host smoke with one success path and one auth rejection path.

required-evidence.evidence-1: VF-LIVE-001, VF-LIVE-002, VF-LIVE-003, and VF-LIVE-005 each produce their required log markers or trace sequence evidence.
required-evidence.evidence-2: The smoke request returns deterministic output metadata such as HTTP status, response headers, or a checksum of a known small payload.
required-evidence.evidence-3: The success-path evidence packet includes the command exit code, bounded log tails, and bound-socket state from both hosts.

LV-002.failure-packet-template: scenario=TwoHostSmokeExecution; expected-evidence=live startup, one successful proxy request, and clean shutdown; observed-evidence=log excerpts, command exit codes, socket state, and remote process state; first-divergent-block=first missing required marker; next-action=repair the failing module before repeating the live wave

LV-003.NAME: LiveAuthRejectExecution
LV-003.goal: Run one explicit bad-auth attempt after the success path and prove the server rejects it without creating a live session.

required-evidence.evidence-1: VF-LIVE-004 produces the auth validation anchor and a deterministic reject trajectory.
required-evidence.evidence-2: The bad-auth attempt leaves no surviving session on the server after the failed request.
required-evidence.evidence-3: The failure is observable without exposing the invalid token value in logs or saved command transcripts.

LV-003.failure-packet-template: scenario=LiveAuthRejectExecution; expected-evidence=explicit bad-auth attempt rejected with redacted evidence and no surviving session; observed-evidence=server log tail, client log tail, bad-auth command exit code, and session or socket state; first-divergent-block=first missing reject-path marker; next-action=repair auth or handshake handling before any further live rerun

LV-004.NAME: LiveEvidenceCollection
LV-004.goal: Collect narrow high-signal artifacts from both hosts so another agent can diagnose failure without rerunning blind.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Logs include only anchored high-signal lines and redact secrets.
required-evidence.evidence-2: Socket listings prove whether listeners were bound at the moment of success or failure.
required-evidence.evidence-3: Captured evidence is small enough for a follow-up fix packet and identifies the first divergent block.
required-evidence.evidence-4: The evidence bundle contains separate success-path and bad-auth-path artifacts so the two trajectories are not conflated.

LV-004.failure-packet-template: scenario=LiveEvidenceCollection; expected-evidence=both host logs plus socket state plus smoke result; observed-evidence=missing or noisy artifacts; first-divergent-block=EVIDENCE_COLLECTION; next-action=improve log capture or restart with explicit output paths

LV-005.NAME: ManagedServiceWavePreparation
LV-005.goal: Prepare the managed deployment shape for the second live wave without falling back to ad hoc host mutation.

required-evidence.evidence-1: The canonical deploy layout, unit names, env sources, and log targets are fixed before managed live verification starts.
required-evidence.evidence-2: No approved command requires raw secret material to be embedded in repository files.

LV-005.failure-packet-template: scenario=ManagedServiceWavePreparation; expected-evidence=deploy script, units, and secret inputs are aligned; observed-evidence=dry-run, static verify, or doc drift failure; first-divergent-block=managed prep artifact mismatch; next-action=repair deploy layout, unit files, or secret inputs before service-managed live execution

LV-006.NAME: ManagedServiceWaveExecution
LV-006.goal: Run the second live wave under managed services with repeated smoke, restart recovery, and bounded bad-auth evidence.

commands.command-1: ssh root@$N0WSS_SERVER_HOST 'systemctl restart n0wss-server && systemctl is-active n0wss-server'
commands.command-2: ssh root@$N0WSS_CLIENT_HOST 'systemctl restart n0wss-client && systemctl is-active n0wss-client'

required-evidence.evidence-1: VF-DEPLOY-002, VF-DEPLOY-003, VF-DEPLOY-004, VF-DEPLOY-005, and VF-DEPLOY-006 each produce their required evidence.
required-evidence.evidence-2: Repeated smoke and restart evidence are captured separately from the bad-auth evidence packet.

LV-006.failure-packet-template: scenario=ManagedServiceWaveExecution; expected-evidence=service-managed readiness, repeated smoke success, restart recovery, and redacted bad-auth reject; observed-evidence=journal, systemctl, socket, and output failure details; first-divergent-block=first missing managed-service marker; next-action=repair service, deploy, or runtime behavior before rerun

LV-007.NAME: TelegramProxyWave
LV-007.goal: Run one Telegram-specific compatibility wave through the governed SOCKS5 endpoint and capture bounded connect plus reconnect evidence.

commands.command-3: if Telegram runs on the local operator workstation, start and keep alive `ssh -N -L 127.0.0.1:$N0WSS_SOCKS5_PORT:127.0.0.1:$N0WSS_SOCKS5_PORT root@$N0WSS_CLIENT_HOST` before the app-side check
commands.command-4: if Telegram runs on the local operator workstation, prove the forwarded local bind with `ss -ltnp | grep \":$N0WSS_SOCKS5_PORT\" || true` before the app-side check
commands.command-5: telegram-client-side configure SOCKS5 proxy 127.0.0.1:$N0WSS_SOCKS5_PORT and attempt initial connect
commands.command-6: collect bounded client and server log tails for the initial connect packet
commands.command-7: restart the Telegram app or trigger one reconnect path through the same SOCKS5 settings
commands.command-8: if reconnect stalls at Connecting on the local workstation, verify the SSH-forward process is still alive before classifying a tunnel-side failure
commands.command-9: collect bounded client and server log tails for the reconnect packet

required-evidence.evidence-1: A Telegram app instance is configured as a standard SOCKS5 client against the local n0wss endpoint.
required-evidence.evidence-2: For the local-workstation variant, the packet includes proof of the SSH local forward and the forwarded local bind, not only the remote managed listener.
required-evidence.evidence-3: The success packet contains SOCKS5 parse, transport selection, and pump markers or a bounded failure packet with the first divergent block.
required-evidence.evidence-4: Reconnect evidence is captured separately from the initial connect attempt.
required-evidence.evidence-5: If the app attempt never reaches n0wss, the packet must explicitly classify the divergence as app-side misconfiguration, forward-liveness failure, or readiness failure rather than tunnel failure.
required-evidence.evidence-6: When reconnect succeeds but a local relaunch attempt shows unstable UI recovery, the packet keeps the reconnect-path success evidence separate from the app-launch divergence note.

LV-007.failure-packet-template: scenario=TelegramProxyWave; expected-evidence=Telegram connects through SOCKS5 and survives one reconnect path with forward proof when the client is local; observed-evidence=Telegram app result, forward state, local listener state, and anchored n0wss logs; first-divergent-block=first missing forward, SOCKS5, or transport marker; next-action=check SSH-forward liveness first for local-workstation runs, then distinguish Telegram misconfig from tunnel-side failure before rerun

LV-008.NAME: FinalTelegramAcceptanceWave
LV-008.goal: Redeploy onto the rebuilt server pair, prove managed readiness, hand off one exact Telegram Desktop path to the operator, and capture separate basic and media-heavy acceptance packets.

commands.command-1: ssh root@$N0WSS_SERVER_HOST 'uname -a && id && command -v bash && command -v systemctl && command -v ss'
commands.command-2: ssh root@$N0WSS_CLIENT_HOST 'uname -a && id && command -v bash && command -v systemctl && command -v ss'
commands.command-4: ssh root@$N0WSS_SERVER_HOST 'systemctl is-active n0wss-server && systemctl show n0wss-server -p MainPID --no-pager'
commands.command-5: ssh root@$N0WSS_CLIENT_HOST 'systemctl is-active n0wss-client && systemctl show n0wss-client -p MainPID --no-pager'
commands.command-8: operator starts and keeps alive `ssh -N -L 127.0.0.1:$N0WSS_SOCKS5_PORT:127.0.0.1:$N0WSS_SOCKS5_PORT root@$N0WSS_CLIENT_HOST` on the local workstation
commands.command-9: operator proves the forwarded local bind with `ss -ltnp | grep \":$N0WSS_SOCKS5_PORT\" || true` before Telegram Desktop testing
commands.command-10: operator configures Telegram Desktop to use SOCKS5 127.0.0.1:$N0WSS_SOCKS5_PORT with no username and no password
commands.command-11: operator performs a basic-connect packet and at least one text or small-message action
commands.command-12: operator performs media-heavy checks as available: photo send, large file send, voice call, and video call
commands.command-13: if Telegram stalls at Connecting or a media path fails, verify SSH-forward liveness before classifying a tunnel-side failure
commands.command-14: capture separate bounded server and client log tails after basic-connect and after media-heavy actions

required-evidence.evidence-1: The fresh-host packet proves SSH reachability, OS identity, and baseline tooling on both rebuilt Ubuntu hosts before deploy.
required-evidence.evidence-2: The deploy packet proves the governed managed path was reapplied to both hosts with no ad hoc drift.
required-evidence.evidence-3: The readiness packet proves active services, bound listeners, and startup anchors before the operator starts Telegram testing.
required-evidence.evidence-4: The handoff packet contains the exact SSH-forward command, SOCKS5 parameters, and the moment the operator was told to begin.
required-evidence.evidence-5: The basic-connect packet is separate from photo, large-file, voice-call, and video-call observations.
required-evidence.evidence-6: Any failure packet identifies whether the first divergence was host baseline, deploy, readiness, SSH-forward, SOCKS5 ingress, transport selection, or a media-specific path.
required-evidence.evidence-7: The final go/no-go decision remains explicitly bounded to Telegram Desktop on the rebuilt host pair.
required-evidence.evidence-8: The acceptance record preserves the March 29, 2026 outcome that text, photo, and large-file paths were green while call attempts stalled at Telegram key exchange.

LV-008.failure-packet-template: scenario=FinalTelegramAcceptanceWave; expected-evidence=fresh-host baseline, managed readiness, explicit SSH-forward handoff, basic-connect packet, and separate media packets; observed-evidence=host baseline output, deploy output, service state, forward state, Telegram operator result, and anchored server-client logs; first-divergent-block=first missing host-baseline, readiness, SSH-forward, SOCKS5, transport, or media marker; next-action=repair the first divergent layer before repeating only the dependent acceptance step

Gate-Phase-1.goal: Foundation modules have deterministic local checks for config, redaction, metrics, auth, and TLS setup.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Startup, metrics, auth, and TLS boundaries have module-local checks defined.
required-evidence.evidence-2: Phase-1 anchors exist for BLOCK_VALIDATE_CONFIGURATION, BLOCK_INIT_TRACING, BLOCK_VALIDATE_HANDSHAKE, BLOCK_LOAD_TLS_MATERIAL, and BLOCK_START_APPLICATION.
required-evidence.evidence-3: No Phase-1 module relies on integration-only transports or end-to-end tunnel setup to prove its local contract.

Gate-Phase-2.goal: Both transport boundaries satisfy the shared adapter cleanup and task-tracking contracts.

required-evidence.evidence-1: WSS and iroh adapter contract tests pass without leaked tracked tasks.

Gate-Phase-3.goal: Session orchestration, typed effect dispatch, fallback, and state transitions are explicit and verifiable.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Registry, selector, pure transitions, and stable top-level effect routing are automated and green.

Gate-Phase-4.goal: SOCKS5 ingress produces bounded ProxyIntent work items and accurate reply codes.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Bounded queue semantics, deterministic request parsing, and exhaustive reply mapping are covered.

Gate-Phase-5.goal: The bridge carries real traffic over transport-agnostic streams.

required-evidence.evidence-1: At least one end-to-end SOCKS5-over-tunnel scenario is automated and green.

Gate-Phase-6.goal: Fallback, shutdown, timing, burst handling, and observability are robust enough for broader execution waves.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Controlled shutdown, queue pressure, session limit pressure, sliding-window burst detection, and transport fallback scenarios produce stable evidence.

Gate-Phase-7.goal: The repository is release-ready for GitHub publication and first-round smoke testing.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Markup debt is removed, release docs are aligned, CI is current, the release binary is emitted, and the smoke path passes.

Gate-Live-1.goal: The first two-host live environment proves remote readiness, one successful proxied request, one deterministic auth rejection, and clean shutdown.

required-evidence.evidence-1: VF-LIVE-001 proves server readiness with config, TLS, and startup anchors.
required-evidence.evidence-2: VF-LIVE-002 proves client readiness with config and startup anchors.
required-evidence.evidence-3: VF-LIVE-003 proves one successful proxied request with transport-selection and pump evidence.
required-evidence.evidence-4: VF-LIVE-004 proves invalid auth is rejected with redacted server-side evidence.
required-evidence.evidence-5: VF-LIVE-005 proves shutdown leaves no live listeners or stuck session cleanup.

Gate-Phase-9.goal: The project is ready for repeatable managed deployment, bounded evidence capture, and a second live verification wave.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Deploy layout, secret handling, deploy scripting, systemd units, and runbook stay aligned.
required-evidence.evidence-2: Managed startup, restart recovery, repeated smoke, and bad-auth checks produce bounded evidence suitable for another agent to debug.
required-evidence.evidence-3: Managed-service evidence includes both system-manager state and runtime anchors, with separate packets for success, restart, and reject trajectories.

Gate-Phase-10.goal: The repository is ready to tag and publish the source-first v0.3.0 release without dirty-tree blockers or release-note drift.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Governed test-file cleanup, release-facing docs, and the v0.3.0 changelog are committed and aligned.
required-evidence.evidence-2: The next release version is explicitly justified against the remote tag baseline.
required-evidence.evidence-3: GRACE review, refresh, and status all support the release-ready decision.
required-evidence.evidence-4: The final release-ready commit has no tracked dirty files and no whitespace or patch-integrity defects.

Gate-Phase-11.goal: The project has bounded Telegram-specific compatibility evidence without overstating unblock guarantees.

required-evidence.evidence-1: Telegram-facing profile, readiness path, smoke wave, and evidence packet shape are documented and aligned.
required-evidence.evidence-2: Telegram compatibility is documented as SOCKS5 proxy support over the existing tunnel path, not as a new protocol.
required-evidence.evidence-3: Compatibility claims stay inside the verified evidence envelope.
required-evidence.evidence-4: Phase evidence includes separable readiness, initial-connect, reconnect, and bounded-failure packets rather than one blended manual transcript.

Gate-Phase-12.goal: The proven local Telegram Desktop path is operator-reproducible because the SSH-forward prerequisite is explicit in both the runbook and verification contract.

commands.command-1: rg -n 'ssh -N -L 127.0.0.1:1080:127.0.0.1:1080|Telegram Desktop|local workstation|remote client host' docs/OPERATORS.md
commands.command-3: rg -n 'M-TELEGRAM-FORWARD-|forwarded Desktop|SSH local forward' docs/knowledge-graph.xml docs/development-plan.xml

required-evidence.evidence-1: The operator runbook makes the local-forward prerequisite explicit for the verified Desktop scenario.
required-evidence.evidence-2: LV-007 mirrors the same prerequisite instead of assuming a local listener exists by itself.
required-evidence.evidence-3: The knowledge graph stays synchronized with the clarified Telegram Desktop operator path.
required-evidence.evidence-4: The failure contract explicitly checks SSH-forward liveness before a local-workstation Connecting-only symptom can be blamed on the tunnel runtime.

Gate-Phase-13.goal: The repository is ready to create and push the v0.3.1 patch-release tag after the Telegram forward clarification wave.

required-evidence.evidence-1: The changelog contains a v0.3.1 entry for the Telegram forward clarification patch scope.
required-evidence.evidence-2: Release-facing docs stay aligned with the verified forwarded Telegram Desktop path.
required-evidence.evidence-3: The local tree is clean and patch-integrity checks pass before tag creation.
required-evidence.evidence-4: The remote baseline already contains v0.3.0 and the next tag is justified as v0.3.1.
required-evidence.evidence-5: The existing GitHub workflow remains valid for the pushed v0.3.1 tag.
required-evidence.evidence-6: No stale local v0.3.1 tag exists before the approved release-push step.

Gate-Phase-14.goal: The rebuilt two-host environment is freshly deployed, operator-ready, and yields a bounded final Telegram Desktop acceptance packet.

commands.command-1: ssh root@$N0WSS_SERVER_HOST 'uname -a && id && command -v bash && command -v systemctl && command -v ss'
commands.command-2: ssh root@$N0WSS_CLIENT_HOST 'uname -a && id && command -v bash && command -v systemctl && command -v ss'

required-evidence.evidence-1: Both rebuilt Ubuntu hosts produce a fresh-host baseline packet before the governed deploy path starts.
required-evidence.evidence-2: Both rebuilt Ubuntu hosts are redeployed and green before the operator Telegram test starts.
required-evidence.evidence-3: The operator receives one exact SSH-forward plus SOCKS5 handoff for Telegram Desktop and proves the local forwarded bind before app testing.
required-evidence.evidence-4: Basic Telegram acceptance and media-heavy scenarios are recorded as separable packets.
required-evidence.evidence-5: A first-divergent-block discipline exists if any Telegram scenario fails, and SSH-forward liveness is checked before tunnel blame on local-workstation runs.
required-evidence.evidence-6: The final go/no-go decision is bounded to the tested Telegram Desktop setup on the rebuilt hosts.

Gate-Phase-15.goal: The repository is ready to publish the current local baseline as v0.3.2 before the UDP calls architecture wave begins.

required-evidence.evidence-1: The changelog contains a bounded v0.3.2 entry.
required-evidence.evidence-2: Release-facing docs describe the verified Telegram Desktop baseline without claiming call support.
required-evidence.evidence-3: GRACE artifacts are synchronized with the release truth.
required-evidence.evidence-4: The repository is clean before tag creation.
required-evidence.evidence-5: The remote baseline already contains v0.3.1 and the next tag is justified as v0.3.2.
required-evidence.evidence-6: No stale local v0.3.2 tag exists before the publish step.
required-evidence.evidence-7: The release truth preserves the verified Telegram Desktop SSH-forwarded path and explicitly leaves Telegram calls for a later UDP-capable phase.

packet-template.phase-1-example: Config invalid-path test expected BLOCK_VALIDATE_CONFIGURATION reject marker but observed panic before marker emission; first divergent block is Config::loadConfig::BLOCK_VALIDATE_CONFIGURATION; next action is harden path validation and add deterministic error assertion.
packet-template.live-example-success-path: LiveFirstProxyRequest expected server and client startup anchors plus BLOCK_SELECT_TRANSPORT and BLOCK_PUMP_BIDIRECTIONAL, but observed client startup only and no server listener evidence; first divergent block is CliApp::run::BLOCK_START_APPLICATION on the server host; next action is repair server launch, TLS paths, or listener bind before repeating the smoke.
packet-template.live-example-auth-reject: LiveAuthReject expected BLOCK_VALIDATE_HANDSHAKE reject evidence and no surviving session, but observed successful transport selection and no reject marker; first divergent block is AuthPolicy::validateHandshake::BLOCK_VALIDATE_HANDSHAKE; next action is inspect live auth configuration and reject-path handling before any rerun.
packet-template.managed-deploy-example: ManagedDeployLayout expected one canonical artifact path plus aligned service and log targets, but observed deploy script targeting /opt/n0wss while the unit file still referenced a different path; first divergent block is DeployLayout::defineFilesystemLayout::BLOCK_DEPLOY_LAYOUT_ALIGNMENT; next action is realign deploy script, unit files, and operator docs before managed rollout.
packet-template.managed-restart-example: ManagedRestartRecovery expected BLOCK_COORDINATE_SHUTDOWN followed by a new managed readiness packet, but observed a restarted unit with no fresh startup anchors and a stale listener; first divergent block is CliApp::coordinateShutdown::BLOCK_COORDINATE_SHUTDOWN; next action is inspect systemd restart behavior, stale process cleanup, and readiness capture before rerun.
packet-template.release-gate-example: ReleaseGate030 expected a clean release-prep tree, v0.3.0 changelog entry, and green local checks, but observed a stale README or changelog and tracked dirty files; first divergent block is ReleaseGate030::runReleaseGate::BLOCK_RELEASE_GATE; next action is finish the remaining release-prep module steps before creating any tag.
packet-template.telegram-wave-example: TelegramProxyWave expected a Telegram client configured as a standard SOCKS5 consumer to reach connectivity through the local n0wss endpoint, but observed no SOCKS5 parse marker or no transport-selection marker after the app-side attempt; first divergent block is TelegramSmoke::runTelegramSmoke::BLOCK_TELEGRAM_SMOKE; next action is distinguish Telegram client misconfiguration from tunnel-side failure before rerun.
packet-template.telegram-launch-example: TelegramProxyWave reconnect expected a fresh Telegram app relaunch plus a new bounded packet, but observed local UI recovery instability while n0wss still emitted fresh SOCKS5 parse, transport-selection, bridge-pump, and accepted-handshake evidence; first divergent block is TelegramDesktop::relaunch::LOCAL_APP_RECOVERY; next action is preserve the reconnect success packet, classify the UI issue as app-side, and avoid misrouting the fix into tunnel runtime code.
packet-template.final-acceptance-example: FinalTelegramAcceptanceWave expected fresh-host baseline, managed readiness, a live SSH-forward, a basic Telegram Desktop connect packet, and separate media packets, but observed Telegram stuck on Connecting with no fresh SOCKS5 ingress marker; first divergent block is FinalTelegramHandoff::prepareFinalTelegramHandoff::BLOCK_FINAL_TELEGRAM_HANDOFF if the local forward is absent, otherwise Socks5Proxy::parseRequest::BLOCK_PARSE_SOCKS5_REQUEST; next action is verify the SSH-forward process and forwarded local bind first, then inspect remote client readiness before repeating only the dependent acceptance step.
packet-template.release-baseline-032-example: ReleaseBaseline032 expected a bounded v0.3.2 changelog entry, release-facing docs that preserve the verified Telegram Desktop SSH-forward path, a clean tree, and a remote baseline ending at v0.3.1, but observed docs or notes implying Telegram calls support or a stale local v0.3.2 tag before publication; first divergent block is ReleaseGate032::runReleaseGate032::BLOCK_RELEASE_GATE_0_3_2; next action is narrow the release truth back to the verified TCP or SOCKS5 envelope, remove the stale tag if present, and rerun only the release gate.
